serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw chicken neck is approximately 50 g, equivalent to one whole neck piece including bone and cartilage, before cooking.

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat content (~7.3 g/100 g) contributing to arterial stiffness, despite low sodium (64 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is low at 64 mg/100 g and does not directly raise blood pressure; however, saturated fat at approximately 7.3 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g threshold associated with arterial stiffness — remove skin before cooking and prefer grilling or roasting over deep-frying to reduce saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate eliminates glycaemic impact, but high total fat (26.24 g/100 g) and saturated fat (~7.3 g/100 g) are calorie-dense and may worsen insulin resistance if consumed frequently
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken neck, raw has zero carbohydrate and will not raise blood glucose; however, its high fat content (26.24 g/100 g) is calorie-dense and frequent consumption may contribute to weight gain and worsened insulin resistance — limit portion size, remove skin, and choose low-fat cooking methods such as grilling or steaming.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~7.3 g/100 g) and cholesterol (99 mg/100 g) elevate LDL cholesterol risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high saturated fat (~7.3 g/100 g) and cholesterol (99 mg/100 g) makes raw chicken neck a high-concern food for cardiovascular disease — individuals with CVD or elevated LDL should limit consumption, always remove the skin, and avoid deep-frying to prevent further increases in atherogenic lipid load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (~7.3 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; no anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saturated fat at approximately 7.3 g/100 g exceeds the rheumatoid arthritis threshold of 5 g/100 g and does not qualify for the MUFA/PUFA carve-out as it is animal-derived; remove skin to reduce saturated fat and avoid deep-frying which generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken neck, raw (HPB Singapore food code F11010030) is the uncooked neck portion of a chicken, comprising dark meat, cartilage, skin, and bone. It displays a pinkish colour in its raw state and is sold whole with bone intact. Per 100 g, it provides 297 kcal of energy, 14.07 g protein, 26.24 g total fat, and 0 g carbohydrate, making it a high-fat, zero-carbohydrate protein source. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 7.3 g/100 g, and cholesterol is 99 mg/100 g. Sodium is low at 64 mg/100 g. Chicken necks are commonly used in broths, soups, and stocks across many cuisines globally, including Chinese, Southeast Asian, and Western cooking traditions. The neck is prized for its collagen-rich cartilage, which dissolves during prolonged cooking to produce a gelatinous, flavourful broth. Clinically, the high saturated fat and cholesterol content raise concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis; individuals with these conditions should remove the skin and avoid deep-frying. The zero-carbohydrate profile means no glycaemic impact, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns, though caloric density warrants portion control for weight management and insulin resistance. It is naturally halal when sourced from halal-certified suppliers.
